manufacturing_locations: United States (Foster City, La Verne, Oceanside, El Segundo, Santa Monica, Frederick)
supply_chain.dependencies: Approximately 91% of product sales in the U.S. and approximately 65% of total worldwide revenues were from three large wholesalers. Some products and materials are manufactured by only one supplier or at only one facility. Suppliers of key components and materials must be named in regulatory applications, and significant delays can occur if qualification of a new supplier is required.
supply_chain.risks: Supply chain disruptions from natural disasters, climate change, geopolitical instability, manufacturing difficulties at third-party CMOs, single-source suppliers, regulatory compliance issues (cGMP), shortages of cancer drugs (carboplatin, cisplatin), dependency on contract manufacturing organizations (CMOs), potential manufacturing interruptions, quality issues, inventory shortages, lot failures, product recalls.

regulatory_risks: FDA approval delays or denials for product candidates
Complete response letters from FDA (e.g., bulevirtide)
Changes in U.S. regulatory policies under new administration
Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) - Medicare drug price negotiation, rebate obligations, Part D benefit restructuring
State legislation regulating pharmaceutical pricing (e.g., Colorado PDAB)
340B program changes and contract pharmacy integrity initiatives
Medicaid rebate calculation changes
Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy (REMS) requirements for CAR T-cell therapies
Class labeling changes (e.g., boxed warning for CAR T-cell therapies)
EU regulations - GDPR, Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive
California climate-related disclosure laws (Climate-Related Financial Risk Act, Climate Corporate Data Accountability Act)
Anti-kickback laws and false claims laws (federal and state)
Foreign Corrupt Practices Act and anti-bribery laws
Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
Executive Order on Preventing Access to Americans' Bulk Sensitive Personal Data
BIOSECURE Act (proposed) - restrictions on contracting with certain foreign-owned biotechnology entities
Pillar Two global minimum corporate tax (15%)
Immigration and work authorization law changes
Product importation programs (e.g., Florida's authorized program to import from Canada)
Compulsory licensing or intellectual property waivers
Trade protection measures (tariffs, embargoes, sanctions, export licensing)
EU member state pricing and reimbursement reviews
cGMP compliance and manufacturing facility inspections
Post-approval clinical study requirements
Accelerated approval withdrawal risks (e.g., Trodelvy urothelial cancer indication)
Patent challenges and ANDA litigation
Antitrust and consumer protection investigations
Government investigations (e.g., U.S. Attorney's Office for Southern District of New York)
Qui tam litigation under False Claims Act
Product liability lawsuits
Climate change and decarbonization commitments (net zero by 2050)

sanction_risks: Trade restrictions and tariffs imposed by U.S. administration
Nationalization or expropriation of assets in foreign jurisdictions
Political instability in regions where Gilead operates (China, Russia, Ukraine, Israel and surrounding areas)
War, terrorism, social unrest impacting operations
